turns.content: I'm trying to add compression to my Axum server but I can't figure out why the middleware seems to break when I try to wrap it around my handler. It feels like I should be able to just chain everything together, but something about how tower_http::compression interacts with the service trait is throwing me off.

The confusion usually comes from expecting compression layers to work exactly like simple filters that you can stack anywhere without thinking about order. Actually, the CompressionLayer needs to be inserted specifically into your service pipeline before your handler runs so it wraps the request and response correctly. Think of it like a security guard at an airport: they need to check the luggage (your data) right as it enters the terminal (the request), not after the flight has already taken off. In Axum, you add CompressionLayer::new().layer() to your service layer before calling .service(your_handler). This ensures that when brotli or gzip processes the data, it happens at the correct point in the flow.

I've been working through the Rust book exercises and am trying to apply some performance concepts from my Java background. My current setup uses axum for routing, but I'm wondering how I should handle response compression efficiently? Specifically, I want to know if there's a built-in way to enable gzip or brotli automatically without writing custom logic.

You're on the right track by thinking about middleware. In the Rust ecosystem, compression is handled via tower_http::compression, which provides CompressionLayer as a ready-made component. This layer can wrap your service and add conditional headers like Content-Encoding based on the Accept-Encoding request header. It supports brotli, gzip, and zstd formats depending on what you configure.

That sounds helpful, but I'm concerned about overhead. If my API has many small responses, will enabling compression actually help or could it slow things down? Is there a way to make this conditional based on response size?

Good catch on the performance concern. The CompressionLayer does support conditional logic via accept_encoding headers and can skip compression for very small payloads if configured correctly. You can also use a custom service that checks response length before applying the middleware to avoid unnecessary processing overhead.
